title: TSMC's Moat Revealed: 93,000 Certified IPs Build a Hundred-Billion-Dollar Ecosystem Barrier
author: Daniel Nenni
text:
Daniel Nenni
Founder
Staff member

Semiconductor research firm SemiAnalysis has released a disruptive perspective, arguing that TSMC's most difficult-to-replicate moat is not its advanced process technology or EUV equipment, but rather the EDA and IP ecosystem it has spent over a decade building. Through its Open Innovation Platform, TSMC has integrated top global EDA vendors, with its certified IP library exploding from 3,000 items in 2010 to 93,000, creating extremely high customer switching costs and design stickiness. With a single re-spin at advanced nodes costing up to $100 million, customers prioritize reducing design risk over pure performance gains. The report concludes that the key challenge for Samsung and Intel in catching up lies not in any single process node, but in the need to rebuild an entire collaborative ecosystem accumulated over decades—a task far more daunting than improving transistor performance.

Semiconductor research firm SemiAnalysis recently dropped a market-shaking conclusion, stating that the most difficult-to-replicate moat for the world's leading foundry, TSMC, is not its widely recognized advanced process technology, extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography equipment, or industry-leading manufacturing yields. Rather, it is the electronic design automation (EDA) and silicon intellectual property (IP) ecosystem that the company has spent over a decade building around its fabs.

SemiAnalysis argues that the market has long attributed TSMC's advantage to performance, power, and area (PPA) optimization. However, the real factor determining customer retention is whether the entire "design risk system" can migrate along with the fab. This system makes customers "unwilling to migrate, and afraid to migrate," creating a positive feedback loop that is extremely difficult to break.
From 3,000 to 93,000: The Stickiness Effect of a 31-Fold Surge in Certified IP​The most tangible manifestation of TSMC's moat lies in its continuously expanding certified IP ecosystem. Through the Open Innovation Platform (OIP), TSMC has integrated top EDA and IP suppliers such as Synopsys, Cadence, Arm, Rambus, and Alphawave into a unified, pre-verified tape-out network.

According to SemiAnalysis statistics, TSMC's certified silicon IP library has surged from approximately 3,000 items in 2010 to 93,000 items last year—a more than 31-fold increase over 15 years. These IPs comprehensively cover key modules such as Serializer/Deserializer (SerDes), High Bandwidth Memory (HBM), high-speed transmission interfaces (PCIe), Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe), memory interfaces, and chiplet interconnects.

These pre-certified IPs significantly reduce the design risk for customers taping out at TSMC, while substantially raising the total cost of switching foundries. SemiAnalysis describes this as a continuously strengthening positive feedback mechanism: "Ecosystem stickiness drives EDA profitability, which in turn attracts more design projects, ultimately solidifying TSMC's market position."
Behind a $18 Billion Market: The EDA Oligopoly Where Three Giants Hold 85%​Supporting this massive IP ecosystem is a highly concentrated EDA industry. SemiAnalysis statistics show that the global EDA and IP market was worth approximately $18 billion last year, and is projected to expand to $28 billion to $30 billion by 2030.

Among the players, the three giants—Synopsys (including its acquisition of Ansys), Cadence, and Siemens EDA—collectively command over 85% market share. Based on fiscal 2025 revenue estimates, Synopsys generates roughly $8 billion, Cadence approximately $5.3 billion, and Siemens EDA around $2.2 billion to $2.5 billion.

Over the past decade, the EDA industry has posted a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of approximately 13%, significantly outpacing the growth rate of semiconductor R&D investment during the same period. Since 2018, the gap has widened further, primarily driven by AI chip development, increasing verification complexity at advanced nodes, and rapidly growing demand for hardware emulation.

Synopsys CEO Sassine Ghazi has noted that the design complexity brought by AI is pushing semiconductor R&D investment as a percentage of sales from roughly 6% toward 9%. This allows EDA vendors to benefit simultaneously from expanding R&D budgets and enhanced pricing power at advanced nodes.
What Locks Customers In Is Not Performance, But $100 Million in Design Risk​SemiAnalysis's analysis points out that in the advanced process era, the key factor determining the competitive landscape of foundries is no longer pure performance metrics, but design risk.

The cost of a single re-spin at an advanced node typically ranges from $50 million to $100 million, and can delay product time-to-market by 6 to 12 months. For large chip design companies, reducing the risk of design failure is far more critical than chasing a few percentage points of PPA improvement.

Modern chip design flows—from register-transfer level (RTL) synthesis and place-and-route to sign-off analysis and physical verification—have formed a highly coupled tool chain. Any change to a core EDA tool may force all subsequent verification processes to be redone. SemiAnalysis states bluntly: "The entire flow itself is the lock-in."

Furthermore, IP modules such as SerDes and HBM certified by TSMC are deeply tied to its process design kits (PDKs). If a customer wishes to migrate a flagship application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) to another foundry, it means rebuilding the EDA tool chain and re-verifying a vast number of IPs. This also explains the fundamental reason why catching up is far more difficult than the market expects for Samsung Foundry and Intel Foundry.
The Butterfly Effect of Intel's Roadmap Adjustment​Even if competitors can narrow the gap on process metrics in the future, they still need to rebuild a collaborative system with EDA and IP suppliers that has been accumulated over decades—a task far more time-consuming than improving transistor performance.

SemiAnalysis cites Intel Foundry as an example, noting that the company shifted its external customer focus from the 18A process to 18A-P and subsequent nodes. This caused some IP originally developed around 18A to be delayed in commercialization, dragging down the IP revenue of related EDA vendors in the process. This case highlights that once a foundry's roadmap is adjusted, the impact extends beyond manufacturing itself, propagating along the EDA and IP ecosystem to the entire industry chain, further reinforcing customer dependence on mature ecosystems.
Ecosystem Warfare: The Real Challenge for Samsung and Intel​SemiAnalysis concludes that TSMC's true competitive barrier is not a single process advantage, but an entire "design risk system" composed of EDA certification, IP verification, and PDKs. Customers will not easily switch fabs just because a competitor claims superior PPA; the willingness to migrate depends on the complete portability of design tools and IP assets.

What Samsung and Intel face is not merely process competition, but an ecosystem war. This ecosystem that makes customers "unwilling to migrate, and afraid to migrate" is TSMC's most difficult-to-replicate moat—and the fundamental source of confidence that truly leaves competitors in despair.
